result:
The day I decided to write without purpose, I sat before a blank screen and felt the familiar clench of anxiety in my chest. There is something terrifying about absolute freedom. It is the vertigo of the open sea, the endless desert with no horizon marker. To write freely, without a plan, without a destination, is to trust that the mind’s currents will carry you somewhere, anywhere, and that the somewhere will be worth the journey. I have always been a planner. Outlines, bullet points, thesis statements scribbled in margins—these were my safety nets. But on that day, I closed my eyes and let my fingers hover over the keys. I told myself: write anything. Write nothing. Write everything. And so I began.

The first words that came were clichés. “It was a dark and stormy night.” I laughed at myself. How many writers have typed that sentence as a joke, a placeholder, a desperate grasp at narrative? But I let it stay. I wrote it again. “It was a dark and stormy night, and the writer sat alone in her room, surrounded by the ghosts of unwritten stories.” Now that was slightly more interesting. The ghosts of unwritten stories—what did they look like? Did they hover like translucent jellyfish, trailing tentacles of half-formed dialogue? Did they sigh in the corners, rustling pages that didn’t exist? I kept typing, following the thread.

I envisioned a room filled with these spectral fragments. There was a ghost named Regret, shaped like a novel I abandoned three years ago, its protagonist frozen mid-sentence, forever waiting for a resolution that would never come. There was a ghost named Doubt, a sharp-edged thing that whispered that every word I wrote was derivative, that no thought I had was original. And there was a quieter ghost, one I didn’t recognize at first—a soft, shimmering presence made of pure possibility. It was the ghost of writing yet to come, the unwritten future that only exists if one persists. I realized then that freewriting is an exorcism and an invocation. You let out the demons to make room for the angels, or at least for the slightly kinder spirits.

I paused and looked at the word count. Only two hundred words. The prompt said two thousand five hundred. Freewriting for a set length feels contradictory—a constraint on freedom. But sometimes constraints are what make freedom possible. The blank page is too vast; the word count is a fence that says: here, within this generous boundary, you can roam. Two thousand five hundred words is roughly ten pages. It’s a long walk, but you can meander. You can stop to examine a pebble, describe its mineral veins, invent a story about the glacier that dropped it there, ponder the glacier’s slow-motion tragedy in a warming world. So I allowed myself to meander.

I thought about the phrase “write freely.” What does it mean to write freely? For me, it means writing without the internal editor, that stern librarian who constantly shushes the playful parts of the mind. The editor who says: that sentence is too long; that word is too pretentious; that idea is too bizarre. Freewriting is the practice of firing the editor, or at least locking them in a supply closet for a while. It’s letting the subconscious speak in its own language, which is often nonlinear, emotional, image-based. When I freewrite, I sometimes unearth memories I’d forgotten. The smell of my grandmother’s kitchen—cumin and burnt sugar—will rise from a random word choice. A childhood fear of the basement stairs will materialize in a metaphor. The mind is a vast associative network; freewriting is the act of plucking a single thread and seeing what unravels.

So I plucked a thread: the phrase “stormy night.” Storms in literature symbolize turmoil, revelation, change. King Lear on the heath, raging against the elements. The thunderstorm in Wuthering Heights that mirrors Heathcliff’s anguish. Storms are nature’s freewriting—chaotic, uncontrolled, but governed by physics. A storm is a system of gradients: pressure, temperature, humidity. The lightning is a sudden connection between sky and earth, a crackling moment of communion. Writing is like that sometimes. You write and write in the dark, and then suddenly a flash illuminates the landscape, and you see where you are, and you write it down before the dark returns.

I recall a storm from my childhood. I was maybe seven, standing on the covered porch of our old house, watching the rain come down in sheets. The wind bent the maple tree until its branches almost touched the ground. I was not afraid; I was exhilarated. My mother called me inside, but I pretended not to hear. I wanted to be part of the storm. I felt a wildness in me that matched the weather, a sense that the orderly world of school and chores was just a thin veneer over something vast and powerful. Freewriting reconnects me to that childhood wildness. It’s the writing equivalent of running outside in a thunderstorm, arms outstretched, daring the lightning.

That memory led to another: a school assignment in fifth grade where we were asked to write a poem. I wrote about a storm, using all the onomatopoeia I could muster—crack, boom, whoosh. My teacher, Mrs. Patterson, wrote in red ink: “Wonderful energy! You have a gift for words.” That comment shaped me. A single sentence of encouragement can change a life. I wonder if Mrs. Patterson knew that. I wonder if she knew that her red ink was a lightning bolt of its own, jumping across the gap between her mind and mine, sparking something that would burn for decades. Freewriting now, I feel gratitude for her. I also feel the weight of that “gift.” A gift implies an obligation. If you have a gift, you must use it, or else you’re squandering something precious. That pressure can be paralyzing. Freewriting is the antidote, because it strips away the obligation, and returns writing to play.

Play is a serious thing. Children learn through play; it is their work. But as adults, we forget how to play. We turn writing into a task, a metric, a product. Freewriting is play. It’s building a sandcastle not to last, but just to enjoy the damp sand in your hands. It’s singing in the shower, not for applause, but for the resonance. When I freewrite, I feel my shoulders drop, my breathing deepen. The tension that comes from trying to be good, to be smart, to be publishable, dissipates. What remains is the simple joy of making sentences, one after another. Subject, verb, object. Or sometimes just fragments. Swooping, diving sentences. Staccato. Rhythms that mimic a heartbeat.

I become aware of my heartbeat now. The steady thump-thump, the biological clock that measures out my life. Each beat a word? If I lived to 80, how many heartbeats? Around three billion. How many words would I write in a lifetime? Far fewer. Many will be forgettable. Emails, grocery lists, text messages. But some, maybe a tiny fraction, will hold a piece of my true self. That’s what I’m chasing now: a piece of my true self, embedded in this freewritten text. It won’t be a masterpiece. It might not even be coherent. But it will be honest. That’s the promise I make to myself when I write without a plan: be honest. Say the thing you’re afraid to say. Even if that thing is: I don’t know what to say. I’m afraid. I’m tired. I’m hopeful. I’m here.

Where is “here”? Literally, I am sitting in a room. There is a cup of coffee to my left, now cold. There is a window to my right, showing a rectangle of grey sky. The room is quiet except for the hum of the computer and the occasional creak of the house settling. This room has witnessed many words. Some days they flow; other days they are squeezed out like the last bit of toothpaste from a tube. Today they are flowing, but with a slow, meandering current, like a river through a wide plain. I read once that the Amazon River takes so long to reach the sea that the water cycles through multiple rainstorms along the way. That feels like this essay—water that keeps evaporating and falling again as rain, the same molecules rearranged into new clouds, new sentences.

Rearranging molecules. That’s what writing is. You take the twenty-six letters of the alphabet, a handful of punctuation marks, and you arrange them into patterns that can make someone laugh, cry, think, change. It’s a kind of magic. I’ve always loved the quote by Albus Dumbledore: “Words are, in my not-so-humble opinion, our most inexhaustible source of magic.” Freewriting is like a magical practice where you don’t control the spell; you just open the door and see what comes through. Sometimes it’s a patronus—a luminous animal made of your happiest memory. Sometimes it’s a boggart—a shape-shifter that becomes your deepest fear. Both are valuable. Both are truth.

My deepest fear as a writer is insignificance. The fear that I will spend my life stringing words together and no one will care, or worse, that I will care too much and be ignored. I think many writers share this fear. We write to be seen, to be heard, to connect. The paradox is that freewriting often produces the most connective material, because it is raw and unfiltered. Yet freewriting is also the writing we are most likely to keep hidden. We publish the polished essays, the edited stories, but the unvarnished ramblings—those we keep in journals with little locks or password-protected files. Why? Because they are too true? Because they show the messiness of our minds? In a world that demands curated perfection, the messy mind is a rebellious thing.

I think of Virginia Woolf’s essays, her “Street Haunting” where she goes out to buy a pencil and describes the city with such fluid consciousness. She was a master of following the mind’s leaps. Her essay “The Death of the Moth” is a freewritten meditation on life and death, sparked by a tiny moth. She didn’t plan to write about mortality that day; she watched a moth, and her mind made the connections. That’s the discipline of freewriting: not just letting the mind wander, but paying attention to the wandering, and capturing it with precision. It’s a paradox: disciplined freedom. You must be free enough to follow associations, but disciplined enough to write them down, to keep the pen moving or the keys clicking.

My keys are clicking now. Click, click, click. A soft, rhythmic sound. My cat has entered the room. She is a grey tabby with green eyes, and she is staring at me as if to say, “Why are you still sitting there?” She doesn’t understand writing. She understands the warmth of a lap, the need for food, the allure of a sunbeam. I envy her sometimes. She lives entirely in the present. She doesn’t worry about word counts or the quality of her prose. She simply is. Freewriting brings me closer to that state of being. When I am truly in the flow, I lose track of time, I lose the sense of a separate self. The writer, the writing, and the words become one process. It’s a meditative state, a trance.

There is a word for that: “flow state,” coined by psychologist Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi. He describes flow as complete absorption in an activity, where time seems to disappear and the activity becomes its own reward. I enter flow most easily when I freewrite, because I’m not striving for a specific outcome. The process itself is the point. I’m not trying to win a race; I’m just running for the joy of movement. The strange thing is that when I write in this state, I often produce my best work—or at least the work that feels most alive. It’s as if the unconscious mind, given free rein, can out-write the conscious mind. It knows rhythms and connections that my conscious editor would dismiss as too weird or too risky.

I remember a writing exercise from a workshop years ago. The instructor told us to write without stopping for ten minutes, starting with the phrase “I remember.” I wrote about my grandfather’s hands. I hadn’t thought about him in years. He was a carpenter, and his hands were covered in scars and calluses. He smelled of sawdust and Old Spice. The memory came flooding back with such vividness that I cried. Freewriting had opened a door I didn’t know was closed. It taught me that we carry our entire lives within us, layered like geological strata, and writing is a way of drilling into those layers. The deeper you go, the older the rock, the more surprising the fossils.

Now I’m thinking about fossils. When I was a child, I wanted to be a paleontologist. I was obsessed with dinosaurs. I could name dozens of them, from Allosaurus to Zephyrosaurus. I loved the idea that bones could hide in stone for millions of years, waiting for someone to discover them. Words are like that. They hide in the stone of our memory, waiting for the right hammer to crack them open. Freewriting is that hammer. You strike the rock, not knowing what’s inside, and sometimes a perfect ammonite spiral falls out—a poem, a story, a forgotten truth.

I just looked at the word count: I’m at around seventeen hundred words. I need eight hundred more. That’s a lot of fossils. But I’m not worried. The beauty of freewriting is that there’s always more. The well doesn’t run dry; it just gets deeper. And if you feel like you’re running out, you just describe the feeling of running out. That’s the trick. Write about the void, and the void becomes your subject. “I have nothing to write about” is itself a writing prompt. Explore that nothingness. What is its texture? Is it a dark, starless void, or a bright, white emptiness? Is it peaceful or terrifying? The nothingness is never truly nothing; it’s the fertile ground from which something grows. In the space between thoughts, creativity sparks. So I embrace the moments of “I don’t know.” They are rest stops on the freeway of thought. I pull over, stretch, and then merge back into the flow.

Merging back in… I’m thinking about flow again, the river metaphor. Heraclitus said you can’t step into the same river twice. This essay, right now, is a river. The me who started it is not the same me who is writing now. I’ve changed, because I’ve remembered Mrs. Patterson, my grandfather, the stormy porch. I’ve excavated fossils. I’ve played. I’ve turned a blank, intimidating prompt into a landscape of words. And I’m still going. The river flows, and I’m in it, swimming, sometimes floating on my back and looking at the grey sky of the ceiling. The ceiling has a tiny crack in the plaster. It looks like a river delta. Everything is a river. Our veins are rivers. Time is a river.

I’m nearing the two-thousand-word mark, and I realize I haven’t talked about the mechanics of freewriting. If someone is reading this and wants to try, here’s what I recommend: set a timer. Start with ten minutes. Put your pen to paper or your fingers to keyboard, and do not stop. Do not edit. Do not backspace. If you make a typo, keep going. Write “I don’t know what to write” over and over until something else emerges. It will. The brain cannot sustain repetition forever; it will get bored and offer you an image, a complaint, a question. Follow it. Trust the process. Don’t judge what comes. Even if it seems silly or dark or stupid. The point is to loosen the soil, not to plant a perfect garden. Later, you can sift through the dirt for seeds. But first, you must dig.

I’ve been digging this whole essay, and I’ve found a few seeds. The importance of encouragement. The wildness of childhood storms. The fossilized memories of loved ones. The feeling of flow. These are themes I return to often, which suggests they are central to who I am. Freewriting reveals our obsessions. It’s a diagnostic tool for the soul. What do you write about when you have no assignment? That’s what matters to you. That’s the raw material of your authentic voice.

Authentic voice. Another phrase that gets thrown around writing classes. I used to think I had to find my voice, like it was a lost coin under the sofa. But voice isn’t found; it’s uncovered. It’s already there, underneath the layers of “should” and “supposed to.” Freewriting strips those layers away. You can’t maintain a pretense when you’re writing fast and free. Your true rhythms, your true vocabulary, your true concerns will surface. My voice, I’m learning, is a bit meandering, a bit lyrical, a bit questioning. It loves metaphors and memories. It’s not tight and punchy. It’s not cool and detached. It’s warm-blooded and introspective. And that’s okay. Freewriting teaches self-acceptance. You learn to accept the words you produce, because they came from you, and you are enough.

I’m over two thousand words now. Only five hundred to go. It feels like the final stretch of a long run. My fingers are a little tired, but my mind is still active, still brimming with more to say. There’s an essay by Anne Lamott, “Shitty First Drafts,” that every writer should read. She argues that all great writing begins with a terrible, messy, freewritten draft. That you have to let yourself write crap before you can write gold. This essay is my shitty first draft, and I’m showing it to you, unedited. It’s terrifying and liberating. I’m breaking the fourth wall between writer and reader. Usually, we hide our process, presenting only the polished gem. But sometimes the rough stone is more interesting. It shows the human hand.

Two thousand two hundred. I’m thinking about endings. How do you end a freewrite? You just stop. But the prompt wants a full two thousand five hundred, so I need to find a landing. A controlled descent. I could circle back to the beginning. The dark and stormy night. The ghost of unwritten stories. That ghost is still here, but it’s changed. It’s no longer a haunting; it’s a companion. The unwritten stories are not failures; they are future friends. They wait patiently for their turn. This essay is one of them, now written. It was unwritten, and now it exists. That’s the power of freewriting: it turns the ghostly into the tangible. It resurrects.

So I’ll let the storm from the beginning return. The storm is still out there, but now I’m

Later, you can sift through the dirt for seeds. But first, you must dig.

I’ve been digging this whole essay, and I’ve found a few seeds. The importance of encouragement. The wildness of childhood storms. The fossilized memories of loved ones. The feeling of flow. These are themes I return to often, which suggests they are central to who I am. Freewriting reveals our obsessions. It’s a diagnostic tool for the soul. What do you write about when you have no assignment? That’s what matters to you. That’s the raw material of your authentic voice.

Authentic voice. Another phrase that gets thrown around writing classes. I used to think I had to find my voice, like it was a lost coin under the sofa. But voice isn’t found; it’s uncovered. It’s already there, underneath the layers of “should” and “supposed to.” Freewriting strips those layers away. You can’t maintain a pretense when you’re writing fast and free. Your true rhythms, your true vocabulary, your true concerns will surface. My voice, I’m learning, is a bit meandering, a bit lyrical, a bit questioning. It loves metaphors and memories. It’s not tight and punchy. It’s not cool and detached. It’s warm-blooded and introspective. And that’s okay. Freewriting teaches self-acceptance. You learn to accept the words you produce, because they came from you, and you are enough.

I’m over two thousand words now. Only five hundred to go. It feels like the final stretch of a long run. My fingers are a little tired, but my mind is still active, still brimming with more to say. There’s an essay by Anne Lamott, “Shitty First Drafts,” that every writer should read. She argues that all great writing begins with a terrible, messy, freewritten draft. That you have to let yourself write crap before you can write gold. This essay is my shitty first draft, and I’m showing it to you, unedited. It’s terrifying and liberating. I’m breaking the fourth wall between writer and reader. Usually, we hide our process, presenting only the polished gem. But sometimes the rough stone is more interesting. It shows the human hand.

Two thousand two hundred. I’m thinking about endings. How do you end a freewrite? You just stop. But the prompt wants a full two thousand five hundred, so I need to find a landing. A controlled descent. I could circle back to the beginning. The dark and stormy night. The ghost of unwritten stories. That ghost is still here, but it’s changed. It’s no longer a haunting; it’s a companion. The unwritten stories are not failures; they are future friends. They wait patiently for their turn. This essay is one of them, now written. It was unwritten, and now it exists. That’s the power of freewriting: it turns the ghostly into the tangible. It resurrects.

So I’ll let the storm from the beginning return. The storm is still out there, but now I’m
